heading: TONUS TERTIUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the Tonus Tertius with Chapter I, "Quid sit sacerdos, et eius officium" (What the priest is and his office), establishing Christ as the supreme and true priest who mediates between God and humanity. Zorzi defines the priest etymologically as "sacra dans" (one who gives sacred things), enumerates seven priestly duties, and focuses in detail on the first three: intercessory prayer for the people (citing Leviticus with Hebrew transliteration "vecaper alav hacohen"), the offering of sacrifices (citing Paul's Letter to the Hebrews and the Letter of James), and the teaching of the Law (citing Haggai). The chapter draws on the Levitical priesthood typologically to illuminate the office of Christ as mediator and high priest.
essay:
# TONUS TERTIUS — Caput I: Quid sit sacerdos, et eius officium

## Opening and Scope

The Tonus Tertius opens with a programmatic declaration of the immensity of its subject matter. Zorzi announces the entrance into a "latissimum campum" — a vast field that exceeds the grasp of human vision — as he undertakes a treatment of the priest, understood above all as Christ, the supreme mediator between God and humanity:

> "Latissimum campum, et qui sese ab oculis nostris sua magnitudine tollit, intramus, dum tractare volumus de sacerdote, qui medium inter Deum et hominem tenet, et est quasi Deus visibilis, et maxime summus, et verus sacerdos Christus, cuius sacerdotium pro virili nostra explicare intendimus."

<cite>p. 1394</cite>

The phrase "quasi Deus visibilis" is charged with Neoplatonic resonance: the priest is the visible image of God among humanity, a mediatorial figure who participates in both the divine and human orders. This positions the priesthood within Zorzi's broader cosmological project of harmonizing levels of being — the priest is the hinge-point, the middle term, in the Neoplatonic triad of divine, intermediate, and material.

## Etymology and Definition of the Priest

Zorzi offers a Latin etymology for "sacerdos" that functions as a theological definition: the priest is "quasi sacra dans" — one who gives sacred things. This is not mere wordplay but a structural claim: the priest's identity is constituted by his role as conduit of the sacred, receiving from the "sacrarium supramundanum" (the supramundane sanctuary) and administering to the people. The language of "sacrarium supramundanum" places this at the level of the celestial or intelligible world, consistent with Zorzi's Neoplatonic cosmology in which the divine emanates downward through hierarchical levels.

The implied logic is that the priest must himself be "mundus, purus, divinus" — clean, pure, and divine — to function properly as this conduit. Zorzi appeals to unnamed "sapientes" (the wise) to judge this necessity, a rhetorical move that invokes the Platonic tradition of philosophical wisdom as arbiter.

## The Seven Priestly Duties

Zorzi announces that the priest has seven duties in particular, though in this opening chapter he treats only the first three. The number seven itself carries symbolic weight throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi* as a cosmologically significant number.

### First Duty: Intercessory Prayer

The first duty is priestly intercession — the priest prays for the people as mediator. Zorzi cites the Book of Leviticus, where in the context of perjury the phrase is repeated twice: "Et rogabit pro eo sacerdos, et dimittetur ei" (And the priest shall pray for him, and he shall be forgiven). Critically, Zorzi supports this with a Hebrew transliteration of the underlying text:

> "ut sonat veritas litterae, quae sic habetur [...] vecaper alav hacohen"

<cite>p. 1394</cite>

The phrase "vecaper alav hacohen" (וְכִפֶּר עָלָיו הַכֹּהֵן) — "and the priest shall make atonement for him" — is given in transliteration, demonstrating Zorzi's consistent Hebraist method of appealing to the "veritas litterae," the truth of the Hebrew letter, against or alongside Latin translation. This kabbalistic-humanist gesture, typical of the Christian Kabbalah tradition running from Pico della Mirandola through Reuchlin to Zorzi himself, grounds typological Christology in the literal Hebrew text.

The same pattern of priestly intercession is found in the laws concerning purification after childbirth: "Et orabit pro ea sacerdos, et mundabitur." Zorzi then cites the Epistle of James (5:14): "Infirmatur quis in vobis, inducat presbyteros ecclesiae, et orent super eum" — establishing continuity between Old Testament priestly intercession and the New Testament presbyterate.

### Second Duty: Offering Sacrifice

The second priestly duty is the offering of sacred gifts and sacrifices. Zorzi cites Paul's Letter to the Hebrews (5:1; 7:27): "Omnis pontifex ad offerendum munera, et hostias constituitur" and "Necesse habent sacerdotes pro suis delictis prius hostias offerre, deinde pro delictis populi." This typological reading of Levitical sacrifice as prefiguring Christ's self-offering is standard in Zorzi's Christological framework.

### Third Duty: Teaching the Law

The third duty is that the priest is appointed as teacher of the people — not of "vanae, et curiosae doctrinae" (vain and curious doctrines) but of the Law and divine things. Zorzi cites the prophet Haggai ("ut in Aggeo dicitur: Interroga sacerdotes legem"), again grounding the priestly teaching office in the Hebrew prophetic tradition.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chapter inaugurates what will be the Tonus Tertius's extended meditation on the priestly office as the central organizing metaphor for Christ's cosmic role as mediator. The chapter's structure — etymological definition, enumeration of duties grounded in Hebrew Scripture, typological movement from Levitical to Christian priesthood — exemplifies Zorzi's synthetic method: Neoplatonic metaphysics of mediation, Christian Kabbalah's appeal to Hebrew textual authority, and Pauline theology converge in the figure of the priest as "medium inter Deum et hominem." The Christological culmination ("maxime summus, et verus sacerdos Christus") frames all subsequent priestly typology as pointing toward the supreme harmonic mediator of the cosmos.
